title: The Light Returns–But What About Trust? Will the Warmth Between Us Fade?
text:
The Light Returns—But What About Trust? Will the Warmth Between Us Fade?

The Light Returns–But What About Trust? Will the Warmth Between Us Fade?

                    Ruslana Moskotina                

                    Mariia Rempen                

Analytics

Society
 

March 18, 2026

 
448

 

Shocking and traumatic events can have ambiguous effects on society: they may either increase our 
trust in the authorities
, 
in one another
, or even our 
life satisfaction
—or 
undermine
 them. By systematically destroying Ukraine’s energy infrastructure, Russia seeks to break our will to resist. The enemy is betting on the accumulation of fatigue and fear, as well as growing mutual hostility within society and rising distrust of the authorities.

 Russia continued striking energy infrastructure in January and February 2026 against the backdrop of both a harsh winter with severe frosts and the so-called negotiations on ending the war. The loss of electricity and heat was meant to exhaust and break us—but did it actually succeed?

This winter became another test of resilience for Ukrainians, and particularly for residents of Kyiv. People’s reactions proved strikingly ambivalent. On the one hand, we saw numerous examples of solidarity: 
people stepped in to help elderly residents
, who found it hardest to cope in cold apartments without electricity or heating. On the other hand, mounting frustration spilled over into 
accusations
 that the authorities had been unprepared for blackouts, and at times even into 
roads being blocked
 over the prolonged lack of heating, which caused inconvenience for other residents.

Such polarized reactions raise important questions: what happened to Kyiv residents’ trust in the authorities and in one another this winter? Are we becoming more indifferent—or, conversely, more willing to help? When access to utilities improves, does this make us more empathetic (because our basic needs are largely met and we can pay more attention to others’ suffering), or less compassionate, since when difficult times recede the perceived need to pull together also seems to fade?

Methodology

To answer these questions, we conducted a panel survey of the same Kyiv residents aged 18 and older, with a one-week interval between the two waves (a detailed description of the sample is provided in the appendix). The first wave of the survey took place on January 31, 2026. By that point, the energy system had already been severely strained by repeated attacks, and after a brief spell of milder weather, freezing temperatures returned to the capital.

Public sentiment was mixed: residents were awaiting new attacks with anxiety, yet at the same time reports circulated about a possible “
energy truce
.” Against this backdrop, some people even began 
canceling orders for generators and power banks
. Yet on January 31 itself, even without missile strikes, a large-scale system failure occurred, leading to the loss of electricity, water, and heating, as well as the shutdown of the metro. It was an event no one expected (while we have become accustomed to scheduled power outages, unpredictability is much harder to get used to). On that very day, we surveyed 542 Kyiv residents (1,346 people received the questionnaire, of whom 560 responded; however, 18 responses were discarded due to suspected automated completion or missing socio-demographic data).

The second wave of the study took place a week later, on February 7. The situation that day was mixed. On the one hand, the frosts had eased, and there were no unforeseen events that negatively affected the energy system. On the other hand, several Russian attacks on the energy system occurred between January 31 and February 7 (one of them on February 7 itself), causing interruptions in electricity supply in Kyiv. For the second wave, we invited all 560 respondents who had participated in the first wave. Of these, 407 completed the survey. After thorough data cleaning (removing responses showing signs of automated completion and observations that had already been excluded at the first-wave stage), the final panel sample consisted of 389 respondents.

The survey was conducted using an online questionnaire. In both waves, respondents were asked identical questions in order to capture changes in their views over time. The study was carried out using the SunFlowerSociology online panel (developed and maintained by the research company Active Group), commissioned by the Center for Sociological Research team at KSE University. More detailed information about the study methodology is available 
here
. 

Access to utilities

Figure 1.
 Availability of electricity, water, and heating at the time of the survey (%)

* The shares sum to more than 100% because respondents could select multiple answer options. Percentages are calculated relative to the number of respondents who answered the question “Which of the following do you currently have?” (N = 389). Services for which the shares differed significantly between waves are shown 
in bold
 (McNemar’s chi-square test, p < 0.05).

Despite the still difficult situation, respondents’ access to utilities was better in the second wave than in the first (Figures 1–2). In particular, the percentage of respondents who had electricity increased from 37.3% to 50.6%; the share with cold water rose from 50.1% to 85.6%; and the share with heating increased from 35% to 57.3%. At the same time, the proportion of respondents who had none of these services decreased from 33.2% to 5.9%. The share of respondents with two or three of the listed services roughly doubled.

Figure 2.
 Total number of utilities available (%)

* The graph is based on an aggregated variable that sums all utilities shown in Figure 1. Its values range from 0 (none of the listed services available) to 4 (all listed services available). The improvement observed in the second wave is statistically significant according to McNemar’s chi-square test (p < 0.05).

Trust and prosocial behavior

Access to utilities among Kyiv residents improved somewhat, but how did levels of trust and prosocial behavior change? As shown in Figure 3, the highest level of trust remained trust in family members, although it declined from 9.01 to 8.5 on a 10-point scale. Trust in neighbors, residents of one’s district, residents of Ukraine, and Ukrainians abroad did not change on average, while trust in other residents of the city even increased slightly. Trust in the President and local authorities declined, whereas trust in the Verkhovna Rada remained unchanged.

The willingness to help others decreased somewhat, although it remained at a fairly high level. Willingness to support an acquaintance or care for a sick person remained virtually unchanged, while willingness to help a stranger declined slightly.

Figure 3.
 Trust and prosocial behavior

* The figure shows changes in the mean values of trust and prosocial behavior.

Labels
: gray text indicates the first wave, blue text the second. Statistically significant changes (p < 0.05, Wilcoxon test) are highlighted in color: a green rectangle indicates an increase, and a red rectangle a decrease. A blue dot indicates that no statistically significant differences between waves were detected. Smaller mean values are shown to the left of the dot, and larger ones to the right.

All trust indicators are measured on a scale from 0 to 10 (higher values indicate greater trust), while prosocial behavior indicators range from 1 to 7 (higher values indicate a greater willingness to help others). Respondents who were undecided were excluded from the calculation of the means; their number varied across variables and waves and ranged from 0 to 14.

Although most indicators of trust and prosocial behavior did not change—which is not surprising, given that these are relatively stable attitudes unlikely to shift substantially within a week—almost all of the changes we do observe point downward. This occurs ag

title: The Aikido of Autocracy: How Disinformation Exploits Democracy’s Openness, and What Ukraine Can Do About It
text:
The Aikido of Autocracy: How Disinformation Exploits Democracy's Openness, and What Ukraine Can Do About It

The Aikido of Autocracy: How Disinformation Exploits Democracy’s Openness, and What Ukraine Can Do About It

                    Dmytro Iarovyi                

Governance
 

March 18, 2026

 
336

 

Authoritarian disinformation does not invent crises, it amplifies the ones already simmering. For Ukraine, the challenge is not simply to counter lies, but to build a society resilient enough to absorb manipulation without fracturing.

The Problem: Democracy as a Vulnerability

Disinformation has become a structural threat to democratic governance – not primarily by spreading outright falsehoods, but by eroding the shared epistemic ground on which democracy depends. Trust in elections, institutions, and public deliberation is not incidental to democracy; it is its foundation. When manipulative information campaigns corrode that trust, they attack the infrastructure of democratic life itself.

This is especially acute in Eastern Europe. Countries like Ukraine, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania operate at the intersection of democratic aspiration and authoritarian pressure. Their openness – the free flow of information, competitive politics, civil society – is simultaneously their greatest strength and their most exploitable vulnerability.

Wardle and Derakhshan¹ capture this through the concept of “information disorders,” which usefully distinguishes between disinformation (false content spread deliberately to cause harm), misinformation (false content shared without intent to harm), and malinformation (genuine information used maliciously). The European Union has since extended this framework² under the label of Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference (FIMI), which emphasizes coordinated actor behavior – impersonation, strategic amplification, leak operations – rather than content alone.

What makes modern authoritarian information operations particularly insidious is their adaptability. As scholars³ have documented, Russia’s information warfare machinery rarely fabricates scandals from nothing. Instead, it finds real fractures – war fatigue, ethnic tensions, distrust of elites – and pries them open. The goal is not to convince audiences of any particular narrative, but to leave them unable to distinguish truth from manipulation, and exhausted by the effort of trying.

As Anne Applebaum warns,⁴ we are witnessing the rise of ‘new Rasputins’ – figures who reject Enlightenment reason in favor of intuitive and affective appeal, making even well-funded fact-checking campaigns ineffective. As she stated in her open lecture in Harvard University in March 2026, the only ways of dealing with them are changing the social media mechanisms, and teaching democratic agents to act in this area more efficiently. 

Why Standard Responses Fall Short

Europe has responded to this challenge with a growing toolkit: media literacy programs, fact-checking networks, platform regulation, strategic communications. These are necessary, but insufficient. Research by Carnegie Endowment⁵ shows that interventions to counter disinformation are often uncertain in effect, context-dependent, and difficult to scale. The problem is not that these tools are wrong – it is that they treat disinformation as a problem of false content, when the deeper challenge is one of eroded trust.

Platforms compound the difficulty. Algorithmic systems optimized for engagement systematically favor emotionally arousing content regardless of its accuracy. Online disinformation spreads approximately six times faster than truthful content.⁶ Social media influencers and content creators – operating with perceived authenticity and massive reach – have become pivotal nodes in this ecosystem, capable of amplifying manipulative narratives far beyond the reach of state-controlled media.

There is also a structural asymmetry at the heart of the problem. Autocracies operate within democratic information spaces unconstrained by the legal and ethical norms that bind democratic actors. This resembles the logic of aikido: authoritarian actors redirect democracy’s own strength – openness, pluralism, freedom of speech – against it.⁷ Democracies cannot respond in kind without becoming what they are trying to defend against.

The Ukrainian Case: Resilience Under Pressure

Ukraine (sadly) offers the world’s most consequential real-time laboratory for studying both disinformation vulnerability and democratic resilience. Following Russia’s full-scale invasion in February 2022, Ukraine’s early communication advantage was striking: a unified national narrative, high public trust in wartime leadership, and an internationally resonant message. This demonstrated that societal resilience – not just military capability – is a determinant of democratic survival.

Yet that advantage has eroded. Russia adapts quickly, exploiting new fault lines as they emerge. Recurring narratives of Western betrayal, elite corruption, and – crucially – the claim that wartime suspension of elections proves Ukraine is no longer a functioning democracy, circulate widely on platforms like Telegram and Facebook. The moderation gap is acute: these platforms frequently lack content reviewers fluent in Ukrainian or sensitive to local context, creating space for pro-Russian actors to operate with relative impunity.⁸

Ukraine’s resilience, then, cannot rest on holding elections under martial law or on a single unified message. It requires the harder, slower work of sustaining trust – among citizens, between institutions, and with international partners – while managing the cognitive exhaustion of prolonged information warfare.

Imperatives for Building Resilience

Drawing on the evidence from Ukraine and the Baltics, several priorities stand out for democratic societies seeking to strengthen their information resilience:

Go beyond media literacy as a technical fix. Critical thinking and fact-checking skills matter, but durable resilience requires emotional literacy – the capacity to recognize when fear, outrage, or identity are being weaponized. Communication education must address affect, not just accuracy.

Prioritize everyday communicative practices over crisis campaigns. Institutional responses to disinformation work best not as one-off counter-narratives but as sustained, transparent, consistent messaging. Democratic actors should model the behavior they want to see – verification, nuance, resistance to performative outrage – in their daily communication.

Recognize influencers as actors in the resilience ecosystem, not just risks to it. Social media influencers hold nodal positions in information networks, with reach that traditional institutions cannot replicate. Rather than treating them solely as vectors of manipulation, democratic systems should invest in training, legal protection, and collaborative infrastructure for those committed to information integrity. Evidence from beyond the region confirms that this approach works when properly supported. During the 2026 polio outbreak in Malawi, UNICEF deployed local “microinfluencers” to challenge vaccine misinformation on social media, maintaining a real-time dashboard that tracked polio-related rumors across platforms. The result was striking: in one district of Blantyre, 45 of 84 initially vaccine-hesitant households agreed to vaccinate their children after a trusted local figure (not a scientist or official) made a personal appeal. As a senior UNICEF polio programme manager observed, scientific arguments often failed where community trust was absent, yet a local influencer’s single endorsement could immediately change behavior.
9
 The lesson applies directly to information resilience in conflict-affected democracies: institutional credibility cannot substitute for embedded community trust, and influencers – when supported and properly oriented — are among the most effective carriers of th

title: Analysis of Ukrainians’ Trust in Public Institutions (2015-2025): Who Ukrainians Trust the Most and the Least
text:
Analysis of Ukrainians’ Trust in Public Institutions (2015-2025): Who Ukrainians Trust the Most and the Least 

Analysis of Ukrainians’ Trust in Public Institutions (2015-2025): Who Ukrainians Trust the Most and the Least

                    Olesia Havron                

                    Yuliia Horodetska                

Society
 

March 17, 2026

 
199

 

Citizens’ trust in government is the foundation of socio-political life, as it facilitates policy implementation, including reforms that Ukraine needs. Drawing on 
surveys
 conducted by the Razumkov Centre, this article analyzes changes in Ukrainians’ trust in institutions between 2015 and 2025.

Let us consider the trust-distrust balance for four types of institutions:

political institutions
: the President of Ukraine, the government, the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine, civil servants, and political parties;

anti-corruption infrastructure
: the National Anti-Corruption Bureau of Ukraine (NABU), the National Agency on Corruption Prevention (NACP), and the Specialized Anti-Corruption Prosecutor’s Office (SAPO);

law enforcement, security, and defense institutions
: the Armed Forces of Ukraine (AFU), the Security Service of Ukraine (SBU), the police, the prosecutor’s office, and the courts;

civic institutions and civil society
: volunteer organizations, civil society organizations, volunteer units, the media, and the church.

The trust-distrust balance is calculated as the difference between responses indicating trust (“completely trust” and “rather trust”) and distrust (“do not trust at all” and “rather do not trust”). A positive value indicates that trust in an institution prevails among citizens; a negative value indicates the opposite.

Figure 1.

Data source: Razumkov Centre, 
“Assessment of the Situation in the Country, Trust in Social Institutions” (2015-2025)

Political institutions
. Trust in the President is noticeably higher than trust in the government, Parliament, the civil service, or political parties (Figure 1). This may be because Ukrainians find it easier to trust a specific person rather than a somewhat abstract institution. As shown in Figure 1, the first surge of trust in government during the period under review occurred in 2019, when Ukrainians extended a significant vote of confidence to “new faces.” A much larger increase in trust occurred in 2022 (the “
rally
-around-the-flag” effect). Trust levels later declined but remain higher than in 2015-–2018. At the same time, only the President has a positive trust-distrust balance.

Figure 2.

Source: Razumkov Centre, “
Assessment of the Situation in the Country, Trust in Social Institutions” (2015-2025)

Anti-corruption institutions
. Figure 2 shows that attitudes toward NABU, SAPO, and NACP are almost identical, apparently because Ukrainians do not see a significant difference between these bodies.

Since their establishment in 2014-2016, these bodies have not enjoyed substantial trust. Over time, however, the share of people who distrust them gradually increased, while the share of those who trust them declined – likely against the backdrop of corruption scandals during those years.

After the full-scale invasion, trust in these bodies, as in other state institutions, increased sharply and then returned roughly to the 2015 level. In 2025, trust in anti-corruption bodies increased (the trust-distrust balance improved from −32% to −22%). One possible reason may have been attempts to undermine the independence of NABU and SAPO, along with public 
protests
 against them.

Figure 3.

Source: Razumkov Centre, 
“Assessment of the Situation in the Country, Trust in Social Institutions” (2015-2025)

Law enforcement, security, and defense institutions
. Trust in these institutions surged in 2022, as did trust in other state institutions. However, unlike the latter, trust in the Armed Forces and the SBU remains significantly higher than before 2022 (though trust in the Armed Forces had been gradually increasing since 2014—from +12.4% in 2015 to +44.7% in 2021). Trust in law enforcement agencies has returned almost to the level observed before the full-scale invasion. At the same time, the National Police (trusted by nearly as many Ukrainians as those who distrust it) looks much better in this respect than the prosecutor’s office and the courts, likely because the police, like the SBU, participate in defending the country.

If we compare Figures 1 and 3, it becomes clear that law enforcement institutions enjoy higher average levels of trust among citizens than political institutions, except for the President. This can also likely be explained by the perceived “usefulness” of these institutions for defense in the eyes of society.

Figure 4.

Source: Razumkov Centre, 
“Assessment of the Situation in the Country, Trust in Social Institutions” (2015-2025)

Civic institutions and civil society
. As shown in Figure 4, unlike most state institutions, civil society organizations consistently maintain a positive trust-distrust balance, with volunteer units and volunteer organizations leading the way.

The trust profile of volunteer organizations and volunteer units resembles the trajectory of trust in the Armed Forces: a surge in 2022 followed by only a slight decline afterward (apparently because of their connection to national defense). Trust in civil society organizations also remains significantly higher than before 2022.

Meanwhile, trust in the church and the media, after a surge in 2022, returned to their previous levels. However, while the church consistently maintains a positive trust-distrust balance, the same cannot be said of the media: the share of people who trust them is roughly equal to those who do not.

Thus, as of 2025, Ukrainians trust the Armed Forces of Ukraine (+88%), volunteer units (+70%), volunteer organizations (+66%), the church (+42%), and the Security Service of Ukraine (+37%) the most.

The greatest distrust is observed toward political institutions (−60%): the Verkhovna Rada, the civil service, and political parties. Such distrust of civilian authorities may slow the implementation of necessary reforms, including those related to EU integration. Therefore, it needs to be addressed, first and foremost, through effective communication.

depositphotos.com

Reform Index intern

title: How Charity Has Changed Compared with 2024: A Study by Zagoriy Foundation
text:
How Charity Has Changed Compared with 2024: A Study by Zagoriy Foundation

How Charity Has Changed Compared with 2024: A Study by Zagoriy Foundation

                    Zagoriy Foundation                

Society
 

March 17, 2026

 
504

 

Charitable giving in Ukraine remains one of the most visible forms of civic engagement during the full-scale war. A new nationwide study of the charity sector, conducted by the 
Zagoriy Foundation
 in partnership with the 
SOCIS
 Centre for Social and Market Research, shows that over the past year, it is not so much the scale of charitable activity that has changed as its nature. Compared 
with 2024
, motivations, levels of trust, and models of support are shifting.

What prevents people from donating, and why is distrust of charitable foundations becoming one of the key barriers? Who are Ukrainians willing to trust today, and how is their willingness to help on a regular basis changing? The answers to these questions follow.

As part of the study, 2,400 Ukrainians aged 18 and older were surveyed (representative sample; telephone interviews), as well as 336 respondents aged 18 and older who had received charitable assistance in the past year. A qualitative study of the barriers to and motivations for charitable giving was conducted through four focus groups. 

The full study can be downloaded 
here
.

From impulse to planned giving: regular donations increase, but participation declines

In 2025, Ukrainians’ participation in charitable activities declined somewhat: while in 
2024
, 86% donated or volunteered, the figure in 2025 was 62% (37.2% reported that they did not have the capacity to help). At the same time, in 2024, 73% of Ukrainians provided financial assistance, whereas in 2025 the share was 47.8%. Donation amounts also decreased (Figure 1): the share of people donating up to UAH 2,000 over a three-month period increased from 63% to 82%.

Despite this, the potential for support remains—58% of Ukrainians plan to continue helping in 2026 (26% regularly, 33% occasionally).

Figure 1. Approximately how much in UAH did you donate over the past three months?

One of the key changes compared with last year is Ukrainians’ shift from situational and emotional support to more planned and consistent giving. The share of donors who make regular donations increased from 7% to 25% (Figure 2). The shift toward regular donations can be seen as a way to structure charitable participation and to define a manageable form of support under conditions of limited resources and emotional exhaustion.

Figure 2. Distribution of donors by frequency of donations

In 2024 and 2025, the questions about the motivations that prompt people to donate or help in other ways were formulated differently. In 2024, the question was “Why did you engage in charitable activities?”, while in 2025, respondents were asked about the motivations of other people and the emotions they experience when helping. Therefore, these questions cannot be compared directly (Figure 3), but it can be concluded that compassion and a sense of duty motivate the largest share of people. Another fairly significant factor is the expectation of reciprocity (“Today I help, and tomorrow someone will help me”).

Interestingly, only 2% of respondents reported helping because of religious beliefs (Figure 3), whereas when asked about others’ motivations, nearly one in five respondents mentioned this factor (Figure 5).

Figure 3. Responses to the question “Why did you engage in charitable activities?”, 2024, % of respondents

Figure 4. Responses to the question “What emotions do you experience when helping?”, 2025, % of respondents

Figure 5. Responses to the question “Why, in your opinion, do people in Ukraine donate and volunteer?”, 2025, % of respondents

Distrust of foundations among the main barriers

The questions about the reasons for not participating in charitable giving in the 2024 and 2025 surveys were also formulated differently. In 2024, respondents were asked about their personal motivations (77% reported a lack of financial or other resources, and only 3% cited distrust of foundations). In contrast, in 2025, respondents assessed why other people might not participate in charitable giving (though they were likely also referring to their own motivations).

In 2025, distrust of foundations (52%) ranked second after lack of resources (59%). Indeed, the share of respondents who trust the major foundations operating in Ukraine (the Red Cross, Come Back Alive, the Serhiy Prytula Foundation, the Akhmetov Foundation, and others) and donate to them declined significantly in 2025 (by 6–20 percentage points) compared with 2024.

The decline in trust in foundations may result from a series of scandals in the sector (for example, 
1
, 
2
), as well as from Russian 
propaganda
. Focus group discussions show that Ukrainians trust most those they know personally and want to see the results of their contributions.

“During our discussions with the focus groups, we noticed that perceptions of transparent reporting differ between foundations and donors. Above all, people want to see the effectiveness of donations. For them, this is measured in destroyed Russian equipment and Russian soldiers eliminated,”
 notes Olena Vitrynska, head of research at Zagoriy Foundation.

The quantitative survey confirms this: 79% prefer personal initiatives, and only 13% prefer large foundations. Of these 13%, more than half (59%) preferred nationwide foundations and 27.5% local ones, whereas in 2024 local foundations significantly outpaced nationwide ones (51% versus 23%). Among all foundations, Ukrainians continue to trust international ones the least.

“The pyramid of trust”

Overall, donors trust most those they know personally. In contrast, the lowest level of trust is directed toward the state and government representatives involved in the distribution of humanitarian assistance.

Wounded service members and the Armed Forces of Ukraine remain top priorities

In response to the question “Who currently needs help the most in Ukraine?”, donors most often mention wounded soldiers (63%), the Armed Forces of Ukraine (52.3%), and people with serious illnesses (44%). The focus on the military and medical rehabilitation has remained stable for the third consecutive year. The share of people helping internally displaced persons (IDPs) is gradually decreasing (from 45% in 2023–24 to 37% in 2025). Social and humanitarian causes (“families of the fallen” and “homeless animals”) occupy middle positions—15–28% donate to these needs. Cultural, educational, and environmental issues remain on the periphery (up to 5%).

The ethical stance of businesses is becoming a factor in people’s choices

“Our annual studies help thousands of nonprofit organizations keep their finger on the pulse, understand the mood of society, and adjust their strategies based on concrete facts rather than hypotheses. This year, however, we also examined another important aspect—whether corporate social responsibility influences consumer choice and whether Ukrainians are ready to ‘vote with their hryvnias’ not only for a product but also for values,”
 comments Iryna Hrytsaiyenko, CEO of Zagoriy Foundation.

Corporate social responsibility has already become an important criterion in consumer choice. The strongest ethical trigger is a company’s behavior during the war, first and foremost, withdrawing from Russia and providing assistance to the Armed Forces of Ukraine. 70.9% of Ukrainians say they would choose the products of a company that has withdrawn from Russia even if its products are more expensive. Only 7% focus exclusively on price. This indicates that for Ukrainians, it is not only the product that matters but also the values behind the company.

Conclusions

The results of the study show that charitable giving in Ukraine is changing. Despite a decline in the frequency and size of donations and growing financial vulnerability among

title: Policy Response to Ukraine’s Economic Challenges
text:
Policy Response to Ukraine’s Economic Challenges

Policy Response to Ukraine’s Economic Challenges

                    Yuriy Gorodnichenko                

                    Tatyana Deryugina                

                    Ilona Sologoub                

Economic policy
 

March 16, 2026

 
538

 

Business conditions during the full-scale war were never easy but the incessant Russian attacks on Ukraine’s infrastructure took the challenges to the next level. According to the Ministry of Economy, Ukraine’s GDP contracted by 1% in January 2026 relative to January 2025. This is hardly surprising given direct damages, blackouts, and other disruptions to production. The key question is what the government can do to address the problem of a shrinking economy.

The standard playbook for a “garden variety” economic contraction is monetary and fiscal stimulus: the central bank lowers interest rates and relaxes lending standards while the government cuts taxes and raises spending. The main idea is to bring output close to its potential as quickly as possible. 

This standard playbook does not apply to Ukraine now. 

First, according to estimates from the National Bank of Ukraine (Figure 1), Ukraine’s economy is operating at full capacity. This means that one can create an expansion but this would result in only a temporary gain accompanied by higher inflation. This surge in inflation would add to already high inflation and inflation expectations (Figure 2). Because inflation–and especially double-digits inflation–is so destabilizing to the economy, one would create a short-term boom at the cost of big problems (pressure on the exchange rate, even higher interest rates, economic slowdown, etc.) in the future. 

Figure 1. Actual and Potential GDP for Ukraine. 

Source: National Bank of Ukraine

Figure 2. Inflation and Inflation Expectations. 

Source: National Bank of Ukraine, Infosapience, SSSU. Dashed line corresponds to actual inflation. Solid lines reflect inflation expectations of various economic actors.

Second, to create a fiscal stimulus, the government must have fiscal space. In other words, the government must be able to finance a higher fiscal deficit. Ukraine does not have the capacity to radically increase domestic borrowing. Access to international private capital markets is shut. The only option is bilateral and multilateral official aid. Ukraine’s allies and donors have strongly suggested that Ukraine should raise taxes and lower spending to stabilize public finances (recall that the fiscal deficit in 2025 was ~25% of GDP). That is, they want Ukraine to have a mild fiscal consolidation, which is the opposite of fiscal stimulus. 

Third, one could try to pressure private banks to increase lending to the economy but, given supply-side constraints, pouring more money into the economy cannot create sustainable economic growth. Instead, it would create more inflationary pressures. Furthermore, it would undermine financial stability: if banks are forced to lend to risky projects, one should expect a wave of non-performing loans. This would make subsequent lending even more difficult and could undermine trust in the banking system. 

In short, broad-based tax cuts or subsidies (e.g., a subsidy to 
any 
business that would make an investment), increases in government spending (e.g., a cash-back program), or cuts in the policy rate of the National Bank of Ukraine would not be a durable solution but would only create more inflation and other challenges for the economy. 

What can be a path forward then? In a nutshell, the focus should be on the root of the problem: the supply side of the economy. 

Consider the supply of energy. There is a need to expand the supply and improve the allocation of existing capacity. The government wants businesses and communities to build decentralized generation of electricity, heat, etc. One should use 
targeted programs
 to this end. This could be a subsidized loan, co-payment grant, or another instrument where the cost of an investment project in 
this specific area
 is cofinanced by the government. Importantly, businesses and communities have to have skin in the game (that is, cover a fraction of the cost themselves) to ensure that the money is well spent. By keeping the programs targeted (that is, you have to use government money to develop a new energy capacity or upgrade/repair existing capacity rather than use it for working capital or building a road), one can dramatically reduce the fiscal cost. 

How to improve efficiency? The economics here is simple: if demand exceeds supply, the price is too low. Furthermore, the current tariff of 
UAH 4.32 per kWh
 for households primarily covers the cost of production for state-owned companies (Energoatom and Ukrhydroenergo) along with transmission and distribution. However, the market price of electricity is estimated to be over 
UAH 7.5 per kWh
. Industry consumers subsidize households. 

Raising the price serves several roles: it directs energy to more productive uses, creates stronger incentives to conserve energy, stimulates more supply, and lowers the fiscal pressure. In contrast, rolling blackouts do not differentiate between high- vs. low-value activities and do not stimulate more supply. A price increase can be supported with other programs that aim to make the supply side more flexible and resilient. For example, a longer-term strategy should include investing in installing smart meters–for industry users first and then for households–to enable more flexibility and differentiation in the future. 

There is of course an important tradeoff between efficiency and equity in this context. If some businesses or households have resources to pay higher prices and thus never face a blackout while others do not, there could be more divisions in the society, which is potentially dangerous during the war when social cohesion is critical for the war effort. Therefore, the “golden middle” is a mix of predictable blackouts and higher prices as well as targeted protection of vulnerable groups. 

In summary, when facing a shrinking economy, it may be tempting to launch a “cashback” program, to give tax holidays, and to ask the central bank for easy money. But this kind of “throwing money at the problem” is wasteful. With many binding constraints and limited space for policy maneuver, the answer should aim to address specific supply-side problems. It will be less expensive, it will not undermine macroeconomic stability, and it will give durable results.
